title: Nettle, herb profile
subtitle: Urtica dioica: the most nutritive wild herb in the British countryside
excerpt: A materia medica profile of stinging nettle leaf, its traditional actions, nutritive properties, therapeutic uses across immune support, women's health, and skin, and safety profile. One of the most versatile and underused herbs available, literally growing in every hedgerow.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), nettle entry. Culpeper, The English Physician (1652). EMA HMPC assessment of Urtica dioica folium (2011). Mittman P. (1990), freeze-dried Urtica dioica pilot study for allergic rhinitis.

glossaryTerms.definition: A herb taken as much for its mineral and vitamin content as its pharmacological action. Nettle is among the most mineral-dense herbs available, a cup of nettle tea provides meaningful amounts of iron, calcium, and magnesium.

content.text: Urtica dioica, the stinging nettle, is a perennial herb found across the whole of Britain and most of the temperate world. The sting is produced by hollow silica hairs on the leaves and stems; it is deactivated by drying, cooking, or blanching. The dried leaf is the medicinal part. Nettle is one of the few herbs that crosses the boundary between food and medicine without qualification, it is also a nutritious vegetable (nettle soup, nettle pesto) and was historically used as a textile fibre. As a
, its mineral content is exceptional: iron, calcium, magnesium, potassium, and silica in forms more bioavailable than most mineral supplements.

Nutritive tonic (high mineral and vitamin content), antihistamine (inhibits histamine formation and mast cell degranulation), astringent (tightens and tones mucous membranes and skin), haemostatic (reduces excessive bleeding, especially the uterine haemostatic use documented by Grieve), diuretic (mild, supportive of kidney function). Culpeper (1652) lists nettle for 'provoke urine and expel gravel'. Maud Grieve gives it as a blood tonic, iron source, and hay fever remedy.

Hay fever and allergic rhinitis (daily infusion throughout pollen season, supported by a pilot trial by Mittman, 1990). Cycle support, nutritive tonic in the luteal phase for iron and mineral replenishment. Mild skin conditions as a topical compress (astringent and anti-inflammatory). Fatigue and low iron as a daily nutritive tonic. Nettle soup and food use for general nutrition. Arthritis support, the urtication tradition (deliberately applying fresh nettles to arthritic joints) is historically documented but not recommended without supervision.

Nettle's nutritional content is well established. The antihistamine mechanism (inhibition of histamine formation, mast cell stabilisation) is documented in in vitro and animal studies. Clinical evidence in humans is limited to a single 1990 pilot trial for hay fever, encouraging but not definitive. The EMA's assessment (2011) confirms traditional use for supportive treatment of joint and urinary conditions; the hay fever and cycle-support uses are traditional rather than EMA-confirmed.

Infusion: 5 g dried leaf per 250 ml water, steeped covered for 10 minutes. One to two cups daily for hay fever prevention and cycle support. Topical compress: 5 g dried leaf in 200 ml water, cooled, applied to skin irritation or eczema patches. Food: young leaves (spring, before flowering) blanched and used as spinach. Fresh nettles: always handle fresh nettles with gloves, the sting is painful and can persist for 30 minutes.

Dried nettle leaf at infusion doses is well tolerated and safe for regular long-term use. Warfarin: nettle contains vitamin K, regular large amounts (more than 2, 3 cups daily) could theoretically affect INR; inform prescriber if starting a regular nettle tea habit while on warfarin. Pregnancy: nettle infusion is widely used in pregnancy as a nutritive tonic and is considered safe at normal tea strength; traditional use includes pregnancy support. No significant drug interactions documented at tea doses. Fresh nettles: handle with gloves; the sting is not dangerous but is unpleasant.
